ontology.foodon_gap_note: No FoodOn term exists specifically for Burmese noodle salad (Khao Swe Thoke). FOODON_00002473 (noodle dish) is the closest parent category.
ontology.foodex2_gap_note: FoodEx2 code A0FHR (Noodle-based dishes) is the closest parent. No specific code exists for Burmese noodle salad in the FoodEx2 hierarchy.
ontology.snomed_ct_gap_note: No SNOMED CT concept found for Burmese noodle salad or Khao Swe Thoke.

serving.natural_language_portion: One standard serving of Burmese noodle salad is approximately 250 g, equivalent to one restaurant plate as typically served in Singapore Burmese eateries.

nutrient_source.title: Noodle dishes, mixed noodle salad — estimated from FAO/INFOODS SEA Food Composition Table and USDA SR Legacy noodle and condiment components
nutrient_source.organization: FAO/INFOODS Southeast Asia Food Composition Table; USDA Agricultural Research Service SR Legacy
nutrient_source.source_match_notes: No exact entry for Burmese noodle salad (Khao Swe Thoke) exists in any approved primary source. Values are estimated by component analysis: cooked wheat noodles (~55%), vegetables and aromatics (~20%), dressing components including fish sauce, sesame oil, lime juice (~15%), and protein garnish such as dried shrimp or egg (~10%). FAO/INFOODS SEA values used for noodle base and condiments; USDA SR Legacy used for cross-validation of macro totals. Values represent a typical restaurant preparation.

gi_source.title: International tables of glycemic index and glycemic load values 2021 — wheat noodles and mixed noodle dishes
gi_source.organization: University of Sydney GI Database; Atkinson FS et al., Diabetes Care 2021
gi_source.source_match_notes: No direct GI measurement exists for Burmese noodle salad (Khao Swe Thoke). GI estimated at 47 based on analogous cold wheat noodle dishes from the University of Sydney GI Database (cooked and cooled wheat noodles GI ~35–52; mixed noodle salad with dressing GI ~45–50). The presence of acid dressing (lime juice, vinegar-like components) and fat (sesame oil) further lowers the effective GI relative to plain boiled noodles. A conservative midpoint estimate of 47 is used. Glycemic load per 250 g serving = (47 × 21.5 × 2.5) / 100 = 25.2 g.

hypertension.key_factor: High sodium content from fish sauce and dried shrimp
hypertension.guidance: Individuals with hypertension should limit portion size or request reduced fish sauce, as a 250 g serving provides approximately 1050 mg sodium, representing 46% of the WHO recommended daily limit of 2300 mg.

type2_diabetes.key_factor: Moderate glycemic load from wheat noodle base
type2_diabetes.guidance: The estimated GI of 47 (low) and glycemic load of approximately 25 g per serving indicate moderate glycemic impact; individuals with Type 2 Diabetes should monitor portion size and pair with additional non-starchy vegetables to blunt postprandial glucose response.

cardiovascular_disease.key_factor: Elevated sodium and moderate fat from sesame oil and dried shrimp
cardiovascular_disease.guidance: The dish contains moderate total fat (4.8 g per 100 g) with a favourable unsaturated-to-saturated ratio, but the high sodium content warrants caution for individuals with cardiovascular disease; choosing smaller portions and lower-sodium condiment alternatives is advised.

llm_training.natural_language_description: Burmese noodle salad, known in Burmese as ခေါက်ဆွဲသုတ် (Khao Swe Thoke or Khauk Swe Thoke), is a traditional Myanmar cold noodle dish that is widely popular as a street food and restaurant staple. The dish is prepared by tossing cooked and cooled wheat or rice noodles with a pungent, tangy dressing made from fish sauce, lime juice, and sesame oil, then garnishing with fried onion, garlic, dried shrimp, fresh coriander, sliced chilli, and optionally roasted chickpea flour and boiled egg. The texture is chewy from the noodles, crunchy from the fried aromatics, and the flavour profile is simultaneously sour, savoury, slightly spicy, and nutty. It is served at room temperature or slightly chilled, making it a refreshing dish in hot climates. Nutritionally, a standard 250 g restaurant serving provides approximately 370 kcal, 13 g protein, 12 g fat, and 54 g carbohydrate. The dish has a low estimated glycemic index of approximately 47, partly due to the acid dressing and fat content slowing gastric emptying. However, sodium content is high due to fish sauce and dried shrimp, making it a concern for individuals managing hypertension or cardiovascular disease. The dish is not suitable for vegetarians or vegans in its traditional form due to fish sauce and dried shrimp. It is a culturally significant dish in Myanmar cuisine and is commonly found in Burmese restaurants in Singapore.

confidence_reason: No exact entry for Burmese noodle salad exists in any approved primary source database. Nutrient values are estimated by component analysis using FAO/INFOODS SEA and USDA SR Legacy data for constituent ingredients. GI is estimated from analogous cold wheat noodle dishes in the University of Sydney GI Database. Confidence is medium rather than low because the component estimation methodology is systematic and cross-validated, and the dish composition is well-documented in culinary literature.
notes: Vitamin D (vitamin_d_mcg) is set to -1 as it is not reported in the FAO/INFOODS SEA or USDA SR Legacy component sources used for this estimation. Selenium (selenium_mcg) is set to -1 as it is not reported in the component sources. Trans fat (trans_fat_g) is 0.0; confirmed zero as the dish uses plant-based sesame oil and no hydrogenated fats are present in the traditional recipe. Nutrient values represent a typical restaurant preparation and may vary by recipe. The cuisine_tag 'Lor Mee' in the input source data appears to be a field survey platform tagging error; Burmese noodle salad is not related to Lor Mee (a Hokkien braised noodle dish). This tag has been disregarded. GI of 47 is an analogous estimate; direct measurement data for this specific dish is not available in approved sources. Glycemic load per serving calculated as (47 × 21.5 × 2.5) / 100 = 25.2 g.
